section.title: Solomon Asks for Wisdom
section.sectionRationale: The Chronicler opens his account of Solomon not with the throne or the palace but with worship: Solomon and all Israel go up to the old tabernacle at Gibeon, and there, before ever a stone of the temple is laid, God appears to him at night. What Solomon asks for, and what God gives in reply, sets the tone for the whole reign that follows.
section.thesis: Solomon's request for wisdom and knowledge to govern God's people, rather than riches or honor for himself, pleases the LORD, who grants both the wisdom asked for and the wealth not asked for, showing that those who seek first the LORD's kingdom find every other good thing given besides.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Solomon and all Israel worship at Gibeon, verses 1 to 6; God's offer and Solomon's request, verses 7 to 10; God's answer and gift, verses 11 to 13; and Solomon's resulting wealth and power, verses 14 to 17.

units.label: Solomon and all Israel worship at Gibeon
units.note: before the throne, the tent of meeting

verses.text: Now Solomon son of David established himself securely over his kingdom, and the LORD his God was with him and highly exalted him.
Then Solomon spoke to all Israel, to the commanders of thousands and of hundreds, to the judges, and to every leader in all Israel, the heads of the families.
And Solomon and the whole assembly went to the high place at Gibeon because it was the location of God's {{ohel|Tent of Meeting}}, which Moses the servant of the LORD had made in the wilderness.
Now David had brought the ark of God from Kiriath-jearim to the place he had prepared for it, because he had pitched a tent for it in Jerusalem.
But the bronze altar made by Bezalel son of Uri, the son of Hur, was in Gibeon before the tabernacle of the LORD. So Solomon and the assembly inquired of Him there.
Solomon offered sacrifices there before the LORD on the bronze altar at the Tent of Meeting, where he offered a thousand burnt offerings.

units.label: God's offer and Solomon's request
units.note: ask, and I will give it to you

verses.text: That night God appeared to Solomon and said, "Ask, and I will give it to you!"
Solomon replied to God: "You have shown much {{chesed|loving devotion}} to my father David, and You have made me king in his place.
Now, O LORD God, let Your promise to my father David be fulfilled. For You have made me king over a people as numerous as the dust of the earth.
Now grant me {{chokmah|wisdom}} and {{daat|knowledge}}, so that I may lead this people. For who is able to govern this great people of Yours?"

units.label: God's answer and gift
units.note: wisdom and knowledge granted, riches besides

verses.text: God said to Solomon, "Since this was in your heart instead of requesting riches or wealth or honor for yourself or death for your enemies, and since you have not even requested long life but have asked for wisdom and knowledge to govern My people over whom I have made you king,
therefore wisdom and knowledge have been granted to you. And I will also give you riches and wealth and honor unlike anything given to the kings before you or after you."
So Solomon went to Jerusalem from the high place in Gibeon, from before the Tent of Meeting, and he reigned over Israel.

units.label: Solomon's wealth and power
units.note: silver and gold as common as stones

verses.text: Solomon accumulated 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horses, which he stationed in the chariot cities and also with him in Jerusalem.
The king made silver and gold as common in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar as abundant as sycamore in the foothills.
Solomon's horses were imported from Egypt and Kue, the royal merchants purchased them from Kue.
A chariot could be imported from Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver, and a horse for a hundred and fifty. Likewise, they exported them to all the kings of the Hittites and to the kings of Aram.

ohel.gloss: Tent of Meeting, the tabernacle Moses made in the wilderness. Before the temple is even begun, Solomon and all Israel gather to worship at this older, portable dwelling place of God, showing that the coming temple stands in continuity with the wilderness worship of the exodus generation, not as a replacement for God's presence but as its next, more permanent home.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, covenant loyalty, steadfast love. Solomon grounds his prayer not in his own worthiness but in the chesed God has already shown David, the same word that anchors Israel's worship throughout Chronicles, above all in the refrain, His loving devotion endures forever.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skill in living rightly and governing well. Solomon asks for chokmah not to be clever or admired but to lead God's people faithfully; this single request becomes the defining gift of his reign and the subject of the queen of Sheba's astonishment in chapter 9.

daat.gloss: Knowledge, discernment. Paired with chokmah throughout the chapter, daat is the practical grasp of how things truly are, so that Solomon's rule would be shaped by reality and truth rather than guesswork or flattery.

basic.context: Solomon has just become king, and before he builds a single wall of the temple, he leads all Israel to worship at Gibeon, where the old tabernacle from the wilderness still stood. There God appears to him at night and makes an astonishing offer, ask for anything. Solomon, remembering God's loving devotion to his father David, asks not for riches or long life but for wisdom and knowledge to govern God's people well. God is so pleased with this request that He grants it, and then adds unmatched wealth and honor besides.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historical encounter that launches Solomon's reign, not a general promise that God grants any believer's request. What is timelessly true is the pattern: those who seek God's kingdom and the good of others above their own advantage find that God supplies far more than they asked. The chapter also quietly shows how good beginnings can still end badly, since this same Solomon's chariots, horses, and gold, the very things later kings are warned against multiplying, Deuteronomy 17:16 to 17, will one day contribute to his downfall.
basic.originalLanguages: Solomon appeals to God's chesed, His loving devotion to David, before asking for chokmah and daat, wisdom and knowledge, to govern well. The pairing of these two Hebrew words describes not mere cleverness but a discerning grasp of how to lead God's people according to His truth, the very gift Israel's wisdom literature and, finally, Christ Himself embody.
basic.typology: Solomon at prayer before the tabernacle, asking for wisdom to shepherd God's people, foreshadows the greater Son of David in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, Colossians 2:3. Where Solomon's wisdom was granted and later compromised by the very riches God added, Christ's wisdom never fails and never turns Him from His Father.
basic.christ: Solomon is called wiser than any king before or after him, yet Scripture points to one greater than Solomon, Matthew 12:42, in whom wisdom is not merely given but who is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30. Solomon prayed for wisdom to govern a single nation for a lifetime; Christ possesses perfect wisdom to govern an everlasting kingdom of every nation.
basic.god: God delights to give generously to those who ask according to His purposes rather than their own comfort. He keeps covenant loyalty across generations, honoring His promise to David through Solomon, and He is willing to be sought, appearing to Solomon at night simply because Solomon and Israel had first come to seek Him at Gibeon.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 3 gives the parallel account of this same night at Gibeon. James 1:5 promises wisdom to whoever asks God in faith. Matthew 6:33 teaches that seeking God's kingdom first brings every other good thing besides. And Colossians 2:3 declares that in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
basic.oneStory: At the very start of his reign, Solomon shows what a heart rightly ordered toward God looks like: worship first, then a request for wisdom to serve others rather than riches to serve himself. God's generous answer, wisdom granted and wealth added besides, previews the pattern Jesus later teaches, seek first the kingdom, and anticipates the true Solomon, Christ, in whom the fullness of wisdom dwells for the good of His people forever.

context.literary: The Chronicler opens Solomon's story at worship rather than at the throne room, deliberately establishing that the golden age of Solomon's wisdom and wealth flows out of a night of prayer at the old tabernacle. The chapter closes with a summary of Solomon's riches that mirrors, almost word for word, the closing summary in chapter 9, framing the whole reign between two reports of unmatched splendor.
context.historical: Gibeon, a few miles northwest of Jerusalem, still held the wilderness tabernacle and its bronze altar, while the ark itself had already been moved to Jerusalem by David, so worship at this moment in Israel's history was split between two locations until the temple would unite them. The scale of Solomon's chariot and horse trade with Egypt and Kue reflects the international commerce that marked the height of the united monarchy.
context.canonical: This chapter parallels 1 Kings 3 to 4 but the Chronicler, writing for the returned exiles, omits Solomon's earlier marriage alliance with Pharaoh's daughter and any hint of compromise, choosing instead to open with unclouded worship and a wholehearted request, setting the theological tone for his entire, temple centered account of Solomon's reign.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the returned exiles, who had no king and no temple, the ideal beginning of a reign: worship before ambition, and a request for wisdom to serve God's people rather than riches to serve oneself, so that they would seek the same priorities under God's rule even without a throne.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a unique historical event, a personal audience with God at night, and does not promise every believer such an experience or unlimited requests. What is prescriptive is the pattern of priority it commends, seeking wisdom to serve others and trusting God with the rest, echoed directly in Jesus' teaching in Matthew 6:33.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler open Solomon's reign with this scene rather than with the succession narrative found in 1 Kings 1 to 2?

views.label: A deliberately idealized portrait
views.case: Writing for exiles who needed hope, the Chronicler selectively omits the palace intrigue and rivalry of 1 Kings and begins instead with worship and a wise, selfless request, presenting Solomon at his best as a pattern for future faithfulness rather than a full biography.

views.label: A theological rather than chronological priority
views.case: The Chronicler consistently orders his material around worship and the temple; opening with Gibeon signals that everything Solomon accomplishes, including the temple itself, flows out of seeking God first, an intentional theological frame rather than an incomplete history.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the placement is purposeful: the Chronicler wants his readers to see that fruitful, wise leadership begins at the altar, not the throne.

study.originalLanguages: Solomon's prayer turns on chesed, the loving devotion God had shown David, which grounds his confidence to ask boldly. What he asks for is chokmah and daat, wisdom and knowledge, words that together describe not abstract intelligence but a discerning skill for governing God's people according to His truth, the very gift later ascribed supremely to Christ, in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, Colossians 2:3.

typology.type: Solomon's request for wisdom to govern God's people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the greater Son of David, in whom the fullness of wisdom and knowledge dwells for the everlasting good of His people, Colossians 2:3, 1 Corinthians 1:30.

typology.type: Wisdom granted and riches added besides
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' teaching that those who seek God's kingdom first will have all else added to them, Matthew 6:33.

study.christ: Solomon is remembered as the wisest king Israel ever had, yet Jesus declares that something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42, for Christ does not merely receive wisdom as a gift for a night but is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, Colossians 2:3. Solomon's wisdom, however great, would later be compromised by the very wealth and foreign alliances God added to him; Christ's wisdom never falters and never leads Him from His Father's will, and He freely gives that wisdom to all who ask Him in faith, James 1:5.
study.god: God is astonishingly generous, inviting Solomon to ask for anything and then granting far more than was requested. He is faithful to His covenant loyalty across generations, honoring His promise to David through this answered prayer to David's son. He is a God who can be sought and found, appearing to Solomon in response to Israel's worship at Gibeon, and He delights in requests that seek the good of others rather than self.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of God's appearance to Solomon at Gibeon and his request for wisdom.
If anyone lacks wisdom, he should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault.
Seek first the kingdom of God, and all these things will be added to you, the pattern Solomon's request anticipates.
In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, the fulfillment of what Solomon only received in part.

study.oneStory: Solomon's reign begins not with ambition but with worship, and his single request, wisdom and knowledge to govern God's people rather than riches for himself, pleases the LORD so much that both the wisdom and unmatched wealth are given. This pattern, seek God's kingdom and its good first and trust Him with everything else, runs through the whole of Scripture and finds its fullest expression in Jesus, the greater Son of David and the very wisdom of God, who gives generously to all who ask.

quiz.q: Where did Solomon and all Israel go to worship at the start of his reign?

options.text: To the high place at Gibeon, the location of the wilderness Tent of Meeting, where Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings.
options.note: Verses 3-6 place the whole assembly at Gibeon before the old tabernacle.

options.text: To the newly built temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The temple had not yet been built; that is the whole reason Solomon is at Gibeon rather than at a completed house of the LORD.

options.text: To Solomon's palace to celebrate his coronation.
options.note: The chapter describes worship at Gibeon, not a palace celebration.

options.text: Nowhere in particular; the text gives no location.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly names Gibeon and the Tent of Meeting.

quiz.q: What did Solomon ask God for when given the chance to ask for anything?

options.text: Wisdom and knowledge to govern God's people well, rather than riches, honor, or long life.
options.note: Verse 10 records this exact, selfless request.

options.text: Riches and wealth to secure his throne.
options.note: God explicitly notes Solomon did not ask for riches or wealth, verse 11, though God granted them anyway.

options.text: The death of his enemies so his reign would be safe.
options.note: Verse 11 specifically says Solomon did not ask for the death of his enemies.

options.text: Nothing; Solomon made no request at all.
options.note: Solomon clearly asked for wisdom and knowledge in verse 10.

quiz.q: How did God respond to Solomon's request?

options.text: He granted the wisdom and knowledge asked for, and added riches, wealth, and honor unlike any king before or after.
options.note: Verses 11-12 record both the granted request and the added blessing.

options.text: He granted wisdom only and withheld any further blessing.
options.note: God granted wisdom, but the text explicitly adds unmatched riches, wealth, and honor as well.

options.text: He refused the request because it was too humble.
options.note: The opposite is true; God was pleased precisely because the request was selfless.

options.text: He gave Solomon long life instead of wisdom.
options.note: God specifically notes Solomon did not ask for long life, and grants wisdom and knowledge instead, not long life.

quiz.q: What does the chapter's closing report of Solomon's chariots, horses, and gold suggest when read alongside the rest of the Torah?

options.text: It shows both the blessing God added and, in light of Deuteronomy 17:16-17, the very things a king was warned not to multiply, a tension the Chronicler leaves for later chapters.
options.note: The wealth is a genuine gift, yet it foreshadows the compromises multiplied horses and wives will later bring.

options.text: It shows only unqualified blessing with no tension at all.
options.note: It is a genuine blessing, but Deuteronomy's warning against a king multiplying horses and wealth gives the report a deeper resonance the reader should notice.

options.text: It proves Solomon disobeyed God from the very start of his reign.
options.note: At this point Solomon is being blessed for a right request; the text does not present this moment itself as disobedience.

options.text: It has no connection to any other part of the Torah.
options.note: Deuteronomy 17:16-17 directly addresses a king multiplying horses, wives, and silver and gold, a clear connection.

quiz.q: How does Solomon's request for wisdom point forward in Scripture?

options.text: To Jesus, who is greater than Solomon and in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, and who freely gives wisdom to those who ask in faith.
options.note: Matthew 12:42, Colossians 2:3, and James 1:5 all draw this line forward from Solomon to Christ.

options.text: To the idea that wisdom is only available to kings and rulers.
options.note: The pattern of seeking and receiving wisdom is not limited to kings; James 1:5 extends it to any believer who asks.

options.text: To a warning that no one should ever ask God for anything.
options.note: The chapter commends Solomon's request; it does not discourage asking God for wisdom.

options.text: It has no connection to the rest of Scripture.
options.note: Multiple New Testament texts explicitly build on this theme of wisdom granted by God.

questions: Solomon worshiped before he asked for anything. What would it look like for you to seek God's presence first, before bringing Him your requests this week?
Solomon asked for wisdom to serve others rather than riches to serve himself. Where in your own life are you tempted to ask God for comfort rather than the ability to serve well?
God gave Solomon far more than he asked for. Where have you seen God's generosity exceed your own requests or expectations?

The chapter's closing list of chariots, horses, and gold hints at future compromise even in the midst of blessing. What blessings in your own life could quietly become a snare if you are not watchful?
Jesus is described as greater than Solomon, the very wisdom of God. What does it mean for you practically to seek His wisdom this week rather than relying only on your own understanding?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote records a textual variant: the Septuagint, Vulgate, and some Hebrew manuscripts read was there before, while the Masoretic Text reads he placed before; the difference does not affect the sense that the bronze altar stood at Gibeon.
Loving devotion renders the Hebrew chesed, translated in most places throughout Scripture with the same phrase; its range of meaning includes love, kindness, faithfulness, and covenant loyalty.
Kue is understood to be a region in Cilicia, in the southeast of Asia Minor, part of the international horse trade the verse describes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 1:1-17. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas or removed.
